# Last Time

::: {.incremental}
+ **File I/O**
+ **Sequential I/O**
+ **Random Access**
:::

::: notes
Recall how stored data survives beyond one run of a program. Arrays shift the focus from external storage to a fixed collection held during one run.
:::

<!-- Slide 1 -->

# Today's Agenda

- Introduction to Arrays
- Array Initialization
- Indexing Array Elements
- Traversing Arrays
- Inputting and Displaying Array Data
- Arrays vs. Vectors
- Performance — Arrays vs. Vectors

::: notes

:::

<!-- Slide 2 -->

{{< include topic_1_intro_arrays.qmd >}}
{{< include topic_2_array_initialization.qmd >}}
{{< include topic_3_indexing_elements.qmd >}}
{{< include topic_4_traversing_arrays.qmd >}}
{{< include topic_5_input_display.qmd >}}
{{< include topic_6_arrays_vectors.qmd >}}
{{< include topic_7_array_performance.qmd >}}

---

# Key Takeaways

- Raw arrays store a fixed number of same-type elements.
- Indexes begin at zero and must remain between `0` and `SIZE - 1`.
- Index loops expose positions; range-based loops visit every declared element.
- A separate count tracks meaningful data in a partially filled array.
- Vectors remain the default when size changes or collection operations matter.

<!-- Slide 97 -->

---

# Next Time

- Processing array contents
- Searching arrays
- Passing arrays to functions

<!-- Slide 98 -->

---

# Appendix

<!-- Slide 99 -->

## Introduction to Arrays: Good Practices

- Give fixed capacities descriptive constant names.
- Keep capacity separate from the logical data count.
- Prefer a vector when fixed capacity is not a real requirement.

<!-- Slide 100 -->

---

## Introduction to Arrays: Common Mistakes

- Expecting an array to grow when more data arrives.
- Treating capacity as proof that every position contains meaningful data.
- Using a runtime variable as a raw-array capacity in standard C++.

<!-- Slide 101 -->

---

## Introduction to Arrays: More Examples

```{.cpp}
const int DAYS = 7;
double rainfall[DAYS];

const int PLAYERS = 5;
int jerseyNumbers[PLAYERS];

const int QUESTIONS = 20;
bool answers[QUESTIONS];
```

<!-- Slide 102 -->

---

## Introduction to Arrays: Practice Problems

1. Declare an array that stores twelve monthly rainfall totals.
2. Identify the element type, name, and capacity in `char codes[8];`.
3. Explain when an array’s capacity and data count might differ.

<!-- Slide 103 -->

---

## Introduction to Arrays: AI Search Terms

- C++ raw array declaration
- C++ compile-time array size
- C++ contiguous array storage
- array capacity versus logical size

<!-- Slide 104 -->

---

## Introduction to Arrays: Questions for Reflection

- What promise does fixed capacity make to the program?
- What information does a raw array not track for you?
- When would dynamic growth be more valuable than fixed storage?

<!-- Slide 105 -->

---

## Array Initialization: Good Practices

- Initialize local arrays before reading them.
- Use `{}` when every element should begin at zero.
- Let the compiler infer capacity when the initializer list defines the complete data set.

<!-- Slide 106 -->

---

## Array Initialization: Common Mistakes

- Reading elements from an uninitialized local array.
- Supplying more initializer values than the declared capacity.
- Trying to assign a new initializer list after declaration.

<!-- Slide 107 -->

---

## Array Initialization: More Examples

```{.cpp}
int first[4] = {2, 4, 6, 8};
int second[4] = {2, 4};
int third[4] = {};
int fourth[] = {2, 4, 6, 8};
```

<!-- Slide 108 -->

---

## Array Initialization: Practice Problems

1. Declare six prices initialized to zero.
2. Predict every value in `int values[5] = {7, 9};`.
3. Infer the capacity of `char grades[] = {'A', 'B', 'C'};`.

<!-- Slide 109 -->

---

## Array Initialization: AI Search Terms

- C++ array initializer list
- C++ partial array initialization
- C++ zero initialize raw array
- C++ infer raw array size

<!-- Slide 110 -->

---

## Array Initialization: Questions for Reflection

- Why does partial initialization safely produce zeros?
- When is inferred capacity clearer than a named constant?
- Why can initialization syntax be used only when the array is created?

<!-- Slide 111 -->

---

## Indexing Array Elements: Good Practices

- Derive the final valid index from the capacity.
- Use one named constant anywhere the capacity controls access.
- Keep user-facing item numbers separate from zero-based indexes.

<!-- Slide 112 -->

---

## Indexing Array Elements: Common Mistakes

- Using index `1` for the first element.
- Treating `SIZE` as the final valid index.
- Confusing an index with the value stored at that index.

<!-- Slide 113 -->

---

## Indexing Array Elements: More Examples

```{.cpp}
int values[4] = {10, 20, 30, 40};

cout << values[0] << '\n';
values[2] = 35;
values[3] += values[1];
```

<!-- Slide 114 -->

---

## Indexing Array Elements: Practice Problems

1. Write the index of the sixth element.
2. For capacity nine, identify the complete valid index range.
3. Trace the array after `values[1] = values[3];`.

<!-- Slide 115 -->

---

## Indexing Array Elements: AI Search Terms

- C++ zero-based array indexing
- C++ subscript operator raw array
- C++ array valid index range
- C++ out-of-bounds undefined behavior

<!-- Slide 116 -->

---

## Indexing Array Elements: Questions for Reflection

- Why is `SIZE - 1` the final valid index?
- What makes an index expression valid?
- Why can an out-of-bounds program appear to work temporarily?

<!-- Slide 117 -->

---

## Traversing Arrays: Good Practices

- Make the loop bound express the valid data range.
- Use a range-based loop when every declared element should be visited.
- Use an index loop when position or logical count matters.

<!-- Slide 118 -->

---

## Traversing Arrays: Common Mistakes

- Writing `i <= SIZE` instead of `i < SIZE`.
- Using a copied range-loop variable when elements must change.
- Traversing full capacity when only part of the array contains data.

<!-- Slide 119 -->

---

## Traversing Arrays: More Examples

```{.cpp}
for (int i = 0; i < dataCount; i++)
{
    cout << i + 1 << ": " << values[i] << '\n';
}

for (int& value : values)
{
    value = 0;
}
```

<!-- Slide 120 -->

---

## Traversing Arrays: Practice Problems

1. Display every value with an index loop.
2. Double every declared element with a range-based loop.
3. Display only the first `dataCount` positions.

<!-- Slide 121 -->

---

## Traversing Arrays: AI Search Terms

- C++ loop through raw array
- C++ range-based for raw array
- C++ range for reference element
- C++ array off-by-one loop

<!-- Slide 122 -->

---

## Traversing Arrays: Questions for Reflection

- What information does an index loop expose that a range loop hides?
- Why does a reference allow a range loop to modify an element?
- Which bound represents partially filled data?

<!-- Slide 123 -->

---

## Inputting and Displaying Array Data: Good Practices

- Validate the requested data count before filling the array.
- Use the same logical count for later processing and display.
- Keep collection, processing, and output passes distinct.

<!-- Slide 124 -->

---

## Inputting and Displaying Array Data: Common Mistakes

- Accepting a data count greater than capacity.
- Displaying every capacity position after entering fewer values.
- Showing zero-based indexes as user-facing item numbers without explanation.

<!-- Slide 125 -->

---

## Inputting and Displaying Array Data: More Examples

```{.cpp}
for (int i = 0; i < dataCount; i++)
{
    cout << "Temperature " << i + 1 << ": ";
    cin >> temperatures[i];
}
```

<!-- Slide 126 -->

---

## Inputting and Displaying Array Data: Practice Problems

1. Fill five quiz scores and display them in reverse order.
2. Ask for a valid count from zero through eight.
3. Display only the meaningful values in a capacity-twenty array.

<!-- Slide 127 -->

---

## Inputting and Displaying Array Data: AI Search Terms

- C++ input values into raw array loop
- C++ partially filled array data count
- C++ validate array element count
- C++ two-pass array processing

<!-- Slide 128 -->

---

## Inputting and Displaying Array Data: Questions for Reflection

- Why store inputs before displaying them?
- Which variable protects a partially filled array from invalid access?
- When could processing occur between the input and display passes?

<!-- Slide 129 -->

---

## Arrays vs. Vectors: Good Practices

- Default to a vector when collection size is a runtime concern.
- Choose an array only when fixed capacity is part of the design.
- Compare equivalent access operations when discussing safety or speed.

<!-- Slide 130 -->

---

## Arrays vs. Vectors: Common Mistakes

- Choosing an array only because it appears simpler to declare.
- Expecting raw arrays to support assignment, comparison, or `.size()`.
- Claiming vector bracket access performs automatic bounds checking.

<!-- Slide 131 -->

---

## Arrays vs. Vectors: More Examples

| Requirement | Better starting choice |
|---|---|
| Five fixed sensor channels | Array |
| Unknown number of survey responses | Vector |
| User can add or remove tasks | Vector |
| Interface requires a raw buffer | Array |

<!-- Slide 132 -->

---

## Arrays vs. Vectors: Practice Problems

1. Choose a container for seven fixed weekday totals.
2. Choose a container for names read until end-of-file.
3. Explain why vector assignment differs from raw-array assignment.

<!-- Slide 133 -->

---

## Arrays vs. Vectors: AI Search Terms

- C++ raw array versus vector
- C++ vector size capacity difference
- C++ vector at versus brackets
- C++ copy raw array element by element

<!-- Slide 134 -->

---

## Arrays vs. Vectors: Questions for Reflection

- Is fixed capacity a benefit or a limitation in this program?
- Which vector operations would the design lose by choosing an array?
- What extra state must a partially filled array maintain?

<!-- Slide 135 -->

---

## Performance — Arrays vs. Vectors: Good Practices

- Choose the correct data model before optimizing.
- Measure optimized builds with realistic workloads.
- Compare equivalent initialization and access behavior.

<!-- Slide 136 -->

---

## Performance — Arrays vs. Vectors: Common Mistakes

- Assuming every array operation is faster than its vector equivalent.
- Timing allocation and traversal together without identifying which cost matters.
- Drawing performance conclusions from a debug build.

<!-- Slide 137 -->

---

## Performance — Arrays vs. Vectors: More Examples

| Measured cost | Relevant question |
|---|---|
| Construction | How often is storage allocated? |
| Growth | How often does capacity change? |
| Traversal | Are equivalent access methods used? |
| Total runtime | Does this code dominate the program? |

<!-- Slide 138 -->

---

## Performance — Arrays vs. Vectors: Practice Problems

1. Identify the unfair work in comparing an uninitialized array with a zero-initialized vector.
2. Design separate timing tests for construction and traversal.
3. Explain why one timing result should not determine every container choice.

<!-- Slide 139 -->

---

## Performance — Arrays vs. Vectors: AI Search Terms

- C++ vector allocation performance
- C++ raw array vector contiguous memory
- C++ benchmark release versus debug
- C++ vector reallocation cost

<!-- Slide 140 -->

---

## Performance — Arrays vs. Vectors: Questions for Reflection

- Which costs come from dynamic sizing rather than element access?
- What evidence would justify replacing a vector with an array?
- When is predictable allocation more important than flexibility?

<!-- Slide 141 -->
